title: Kṛṣṇa-Yajurveda
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: c. 1200-900 BCE (oral composition); recensions stabilized later
summary: The 'Black' Yajurveda is one of the two main recensions of the Yajurveda, the Veda of sacrificial formulas (yajus) used by the adhvaryu priest in the performance of Vedic ritual. Distinctively, the Kṛṣṇa ('black') recension intermixes mantra (verse formulas) with prose Brahmana-style ritual exegesis directly within the saṃhitā, in contrast to the 'White' (Śukla) Yajurveda which separates them. Its surviving schools include the Taittirīya, Maitrāyaṇī, Kāṭhaka and Kapiṣṭhala saṃhitās.
history: Transmitted orally with extraordinary fidelity through priestly schools (śākhā), the Kṛṣṇa-Yajurveda predates and conditioned the development of the later Vedic ritual and Brahmana literature. The label 'black/unarranged' versus the 'white/well-arranged' Śukla recension reflects a traditional account (Yājñavalkya's separation) of the differing organization of mantra and explanatory prose.

title: Kṛṣṇa-Yajurveda: Taittirīya-Prātiśākhya
author: anonymous/traditional (attributed in tradition to Ātreya/Vālmīki schools)
date_estimate: c. mid-1st millennium BCE (before Pāṇini in its core)
summary: A prātiśākhya is a treatise on the phonetics, sandhi (euphonic combination), accent, and correct recitation peculiar to a particular Vedic school (śākhā). The Taittirīya-Prātiśākhya governs the recitation of the Taittirīya recension of the Kṛṣṇa (Black) Yajurveda, specifying how the continuous (saṃhitā) text relates to its word-by-word (pada) analysis. It is a key early document of Indian phonetic science (śikṣā).
history: The prātiśākhyas are among the oldest extant works of systematic Indian linguistic analysis, predating or paralleling the formal grammar of Pāṇini, and they preserve precise descriptions of articulation that helped guarantee accurate oral transmission of the Veda. The Taittirīya-Prātiśākhya is attached specifically to the Taittirīya school.

title: Śukla-Yajurveda
author: anonymous/traditional (associated with the sage Yājñavalkya)
date_estimate: c. 1100-800 BCE (oral composition); slightly later arrangement than Kṛṣṇa recension
summary: The 'White' Yajurveda is the recension of the Yajurveda in which the sacrificial mantras (the Vājasaneyi-Saṃhitā) are kept separate from their Brahmana prose explanation, the latter relegated to the large Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa. It supplies the adhvaryu priest's formulas for the solemn (śrauta) rituals. Its two surviving schools are the Mādhyandina and the Kāṇva.
history: Tradition (e.g., the Viṣṇu Purāṇa) ascribes the 'white/well-arranged' recension to the sage Yājñavalkya, who is said to have received it anew after parting from his teacher Vaiśampāyana of the Black Yajurveda tradition. Its associated Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa and the Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad make this branch especially important for ritual and early philosophical thought.

title: Śukla-Yajurveda
author: anonymous/traditional (associated with the sage Yājñavalkya)
date_estimate: c. 1100-800 BCE (oral composition)
summary: A second project record for the 'White' Yajurveda (likely a different recension/encoding, e.g. Mādhyandina vs. Kāṇva, or a separate digital edition). It is the recension of the Yajurveda separating sacrificial mantras (Vājasaneyi-Saṃhitā) from their ritual exegesis, which is housed in the Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa, providing the adhvaryu priest's formulas for śrauta sacrifice.
history: Identical in substance to the other Śukla-Yajurveda entry; the duplication in the project metadata probably reflects two recensions or two source editions of the same Veda. Traditionally credited to Yājñavalkya, whose lineage is foundational to the ritual and Upaniṣadic literature attached to this branch.

title: Bhaiṣajyavastu
author: anonymous/traditional (compiled within the Mūlasarvāstivāda saṅgha)
date_estimate: c. 1st-5th c. CE (compilation of the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya)
summary: The Bhaiṣajyavastu, 'Chapter on Medicines,' is the sixth section of the Vinayavastu of the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya, the monastic code of one of the most influential Indian Buddhist schools. Nominally about monastic regulations governing permitted medicines, it ranges far beyond, containing lists of medicinal substances alongside numerous stories of the Buddha and his disciples, an account of the Buddha quelling an epidemic and converting a nāga, and former-life (jātaka) narratives.
history: Part of the vast Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya, which circulated across the Indian subcontinent and into Southeast Asia by the late seventh century and was preserved in Sanskrit (notably the Gilgit manuscripts), Tibetan, and Chinese. The Sanskrit text has been critically studied and translated in modern scholarship (e.g., by Fumi Yao and the 84000 project).

title: Prajñāpāramitāhṛdayasūtra
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: c. 5th-7th c. CE (short text; disputed, possibly redacted in China then back-translated per Jan Nattier)
summary: The 'Heart Sūtra' is the most concise and most famous of the Perfection of Wisdom (Prajñāpāramitā) texts. In a dialogue framed around Avalokiteśvara addressing Śāriputra, it distills the doctrine of emptiness (śūnyatā): that the five aggregates are empty, encapsulated in the formula 'form is emptiness, emptiness is form,' and culminating in the mantra 'gate gate pāragate pārasaṃgate bodhi svāhā.'
history: Among the most influential and widely recited Buddhist texts in East Asian and Tibetan traditions, it exists in shorter and longer recensions. Jan Nattier's influential 1992 study argued the short text may have been compiled in China from Kumārajīva's translation of the Large Prajñāpāramitā and then rendered into Sanskrit, a thesis still debated among scholars.

title: Rāṣṭrapālaparipṛcchā [Rāṣṭrapālaparipṛcchāsūtra]
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: core by c. 1st-2nd c. CE (in Chinese by 270 CE); later strata added
summary: 'The Questions of Rāṣṭrapāla,' an early Mahāyāna sūtra included in the Ratnakūṭa collection, in which the bodhisattva Rāṣṭrapāla questions the Buddha about the bodhisattva path. It exalts the rigorous forest-dwelling, ascetic bodhisattva ideal and sharply criticizes lax, worldly monks, framing the decline of the Dharma as a result of monastic corruption.
history: An important witness to the early formation of the Mahāyāna: passages have parallels in Dharmarakṣa's Chinese translation of 270 CE, and at least two textual strata are identifiable across the Sanskrit, Chinese, and Tibetan recensions, with roughly half the later Sanskrit/Tibetan material absent from the earliest Chinese. The earliest complete Sanskrit witness is a 1661 Nepalese manuscript edited by Louis Finot (1901); Daniel Boucher produced a major study and translation.

title: Āyuḥparyantasūtra
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: early-mid 1st millennium CE for the Sanskrit recension (doctrinally early Buddhist material)
summary: The 'Sūtra on the Limits/Span of Life' (āyuḥparyanta) is an early Buddhist scholastic discourse enumerating the lifespans of beings across the various realms and classes of existence in Buddhist cosmology, from humans through the deities of the desire, form, and formless realms. It is essentially a cosmological-Abhidharma list-sūtra detailing the duration of life at each level of rebirth.
history: Preserved in Sanskrit fragments (from Central Asian finds) and in Tibetan, the text is associated with the Sarvāstivāda/Mūlasarvāstivāda transmission and reflects the systematic interest in cosmology characteristic of that tradition's Abhidharma. It has been edited from Central Asian manuscript remains.

title: Arthaviniścayasūtra + Nibandhana
author: sūtra anonymous; commentary (Nibandhana) by Bhikṣu Vīryaśrīdatta of Nālandā
date_estimate: sūtra c. 1st millennium CE; Nibandhana commentary c. 770 CE
summary: The Arthaviniścayasūtra ('Sūtra on the Ascertainment of Meanings') is a doctrinal compendium presenting and defining the principal numerical categories of Buddhist teaching (the five aggregates, twelve sense-bases, eighteen elements, dependent origination, four noble truths, faculties, etc.) in catechism-like fashion. This record pairs the sūtra with its Nibandhana, the detailed scholastic commentary by Vīryaśrīdatta of Śrī-Nālandāvihāra.
history: The Nibandhana was written by Bhikṣu Vīryaśrīdatta of Nālandā, conventionally dated to around 770 CE (in the reign of a King Dharmapāla). The sūtra and commentary were critically edited by N. H. Samtani (Tibetan Sanskrit Works Series, 1971), an edition that remains standard.

title: Arthaviniścayasūtra
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: c. 1st half of the 1st millennium CE
summary: The 'Sūtra on the Ascertainment of Meanings,' a systematic doctrinal digest that lists and briefly defines the core enumerated categories of Buddhist teaching: the five skandhas, eighteen dhātus, twelve āyatanas, dependent origination, the four noble truths, the faculties, the limbs of awakening, and the noble eightfold path. It functions as a compact catechism of fundamental Abhidharma-style topics.
history: Transmitted in Sanskrit and Tibetan, the text became the basis for Vīryaśrīdatta's eighth-century Nibandhana commentary at Nālandā. N. H. Samtani's 1971 critical edition established the standard text of both sūtra and commentary.

title: Lalitavistara [Lalitavistarasūtra]
author: anonymous/traditional
genre: sūtra / sacred biography (buddhacarita-type), mixed prose and verse
date_estimate: c. 1st-3rd c. CE (composite; redactions continuing into mid-1st millennium)
summary: The Lalitavistara, 'The Play in Full' (the 'extensive account of the sport/display' of the Buddha), is an elaborate, semi-legendary biography of Śākyamuni from his decision to descend from Tuṣita heaven through his birth, youth, renunciation, awakening under the Bodhi tree, and first turning of the wheel of the Dharma at Sārnāth. It presents the Buddha's life as the deliberate, miraculous display (lalita) of an already-awakened being.
history: A composite work showing a Mahāyāna reworking of older Sarvāstivāda biographical traditions, it was translated into Chinese and Tibetan and was hugely influential on Buddhist art, notably the narrative reliefs of Borobudur. Its dating is layered; portions are early while the received Sanskrit text reflects later redaction.

title: Nairātmyaparipṛcchāmahāyānasūtra
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: mid-1st millennium CE (in Sanskrit/Tibetan transmission)
summary: 'The Mahāyāna Sūtra of Questions on Selflessness/Non-Self' is a dialogue sūtra centered on the doctrine of nairātmya (anātman), the absence of a permanent, independent self. It expounds, in question-and-answer form, the Mahāyāna understanding that neither the person nor phenomena possess any intrinsic self-nature, linking the teaching to emptiness (śūnyatā).
history: A relatively short Mahāyāna scripture preserved chiefly in Sanskrit and Tibetan, it belongs to the broad body of 'questions' (paripṛcchā) sūtras that frame doctrinal exposition as inquiry. It is comparatively minor and less studied than the major Prajñāpāramitā or Ratnakūṭa texts.

title: Śālistambasūtra
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: c. 1st-2nd c. CE
summary: The 'Rice Seedling Sūtra' takes as its image a stalk of rice growing from a seed to illustrate and expound the doctrine of dependent origination (pratītyasamutpāda). Through the analogy of seed, sprout, and plant, it teaches both the outer (causal) and inner (psychological, twelvefold) chains of conditioned arising, framed as a teaching prompted by the Buddha's gaze at a rice plant and elaborated by Maitreya to Śāriputra.
history: One of the most important Mahāyāna sūtras on causation, it is heavily cited in Madhyamaka literature and in commentaries attributed to figures associated with Nāgārjuna's tradition (e.g., the Śālistamba commentary literature). Preserved in Sanskrit fragments, Tibetan, and Chinese, it served as a key scriptural anchor for the doctrine of dependent origination.

title: Samādhirājasūtra
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: c. 2nd-6th c. CE (composite; core early, expanded over centuries)
summary: The 'King of Samādhis Sūtra' (also Candrapradīpasūtra) centers on a meditative absorption (samādhi) said to encompass all dharmas, taught by the Buddha to the youth Candraprabha. It expounds emptiness, the equality of all phenomena, and the bodhisattva's conduct, weaving together doctrine, jātaka-like narratives, and praises of the samādhi that 'reveals the sameness of the nature of all phenomena.'
history: A major Mahāyāna scripture especially influential in Tibetan Buddhism (frequently cited by the Kagyu Mahāmudrā tradition and used by Madhyamaka authors such as Candrakīrti), it grew by accretion over several centuries. It survives in Sanskrit, Tibetan, and Chinese.

title: Saddharmapuṇḍarīkasūtra
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: c. 1st c. BCE-2nd c. CE (composite, in strata)
summary: The 'Lotus Sūtra' ('White Lotus of the True Dharma') is one of the most influential Mahāyāna scriptures, proclaiming the doctrine of the One Vehicle (ekayāna): that the apparent three vehicles (śrāvaka, pratyekabuddha, bodhisattva) are skillful means (upāya) and that all beings are destined for full buddhahood. Famous for its parables (the burning house, the prodigal son) and for revealing the Buddha's immeasurable lifespan and transcendent nature.
history: Composed in layers over the first centuries CE, it became foundational for East Asian Buddhism, spawning the Tiantai and Nichiren schools, and was translated into Chinese most famously by Kumārajīva (406 CE). Sanskrit witnesses include the Gilgit and Nepalese manuscript traditions and the Central Asian (Kashgar) recension.

title: Vimalakīrtinirdeśasūtra [Vimalakīrtinirdeśa]
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: c. 1st-2nd c. CE
summary: 'The Teaching of Vimalakīrti' is a Mahāyāna sūtra whose protagonist is the wealthy lay bodhisattva Vimalakīrti, who feigns illness to draw the Buddha's disciples and bodhisattvas into debate, repeatedly outshining them in wisdom. It celebrates the layman's attainment, the non-duality of phenomena (culminating in Vimalakīrti's famous 'thunderous silence' on non-duality), emptiness, and skillful means.
history: Long known only through Chinese and Tibetan translations, the Sanskrit text was rediscovered in 1999 in the Potala Palace by a Japanese team and published in 2006. Hugely influential in East Asian Buddhism, especially Chan/Zen, for its valorization of lay practice and its witty deconstruction of dualistic thinking.

title: Jñānālokālaṃkāra sūtra [Sarvabuddhaviṣayāvatārajñānālokālaṃkāra nāma mahāyānasūtra]
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: by c. 5th c. CE (in Chinese by 501 CE); likely composed 3rd-5th c. CE
summary: The 'Ornament of the Light of Wisdom that Enters the Domain of All Buddhas' (Sarvabuddhaviṣayāvatāra-jñānālokālaṃkāra) is a Mahāyāna sūtra expounding the nature of buddha-wisdom (jñāna) and the awakened state, emphasizing the non-origination and luminous nature of mind and reality. It is associated with the tathāgatagarbha (buddha-nature) and Yogācāra-influenced stream of Mahāyāna thought.
history: A Chinese translation existed by 501 CE, establishing a firm terminus ante quem, and the sūtra is cited in influential tathāgatagarbha treatises such as the Ratnagotravibhāga, making it an important scriptural source for buddha-nature doctrine. A modern Sanskrit edition appeared in 2004 alongside studies at the University of Vienna.

title: Mahākarmavibhaṅga
author: anonymous/traditional
genre: sūtra (doctrinal discourse on karma) with avadāna illustrations
date_estimate: early-mid 1st millennium CE (expanding earlier Āgama material)
summary: The 'Great Exposition of Karma' (Mahākarmavibhaṅga) is a discourse, addressed to the brahmin youth Śuka Taudeyaputra, analyzing in detail how specific actions produce specific karmic results across rebirths. It greatly expands the framework of the Pāli Cūḷakammavibhaṅga-sutta: where the Pāli text treats fourteen deeds and results, the Sanskrit Mahākarmavibhaṅga adds dozens more sections, illustrated with narrative examples.
history: The Sanskrit manuscript was discovered in Nepal by Sylvain Lévi, who published the editio princeps with French translation in 1932. The text represents a developed, narrative-rich elaboration of canonical teaching on the mechanics of karma and was influential in Buddhist art (its lists are linked to the hidden base reliefs of Borobudur).

title: Arthaviniścayasūtra
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: c. 1st half of the 1st millennium CE
summary: A further project record of the 'Sūtra on the Ascertainment of Meanings,' the doctrinal digest enumerating and defining the principal categories of Buddhist teaching: the five aggregates, the eighteen elements, the twelve sense-bases, dependent origination, the four noble truths, the faculties, the factors of awakening, and the eightfold path. It serves as a compact systematic summary of foundational doctrine.
history: This is a duplicate title in the batch (cf. K10bsu005_u and K10arthaviniscaya), reflecting a different digital edition or recension of the same sūtra. The work became the base text for Vīryaśrīdatta's eighth-century Nibandhana commentary and was critically edited by N. H. Samtani (1971).

title: Āryābuddhabalādhānaprātihāryavikurvaṇanirdeśanāma mahāyānasūtra
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: mid-1st millennium CE
summary: 'The Noble Mahāyāna Sūtra: The Teaching on the Extraordinary Transformation that is the Miracle of Attaining the Buddha's Powers' (Buddhabalādhāna-prātihārya-vikurvaṇa-nirdeśa). In it the Buddha thrice displays supernatural power: transporting his entire assembly to Vārāṇasī; inciting Avalokiteśvara and Vajrapāṇi to gather all beings ripe for awakening and making the world appear as a pure realm like Sukhāvatī; and, with Mañjuśrī's help through a series of dialogues, manifesting innumerable buddhas around him. It teaches that a tathāgata's powers, like a physician's skills, are deployed as skillful means to liberate beings.
history: A Mahāyāna sūtra preserved in Tibetan (Toh 186) and translated into English by the 84000 project. It belongs to the genre of sūtras showcasing the Buddha's miraculous displays (prātihārya) and pure-land imagery, with prominent roles for the bodhisattvas Avalokiteśvara, Vajrapāṇi, and Mañjuśrī.
